calendar.matters:
Real Party In Interest Motion To Quash
Off Calendar Oct-29-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Daniel Bornstein of the law offices of Bornstein Law (daniel@bornstein.law) appears as counsel for moving party, petitioner Andina Property LLC

Attorney, Paul J. Katz of the law offices of KATZ APPELLATE LAW (paul@katzappellatelaw.com) appears as CO-counsel for moving party, petitioner Andina Property LLC

Attorney, Wayne K. Snodgrass, Deputy City Attorney of the San Francisco City Attorney's Office (wayne.snodgrass@sfgov.org) appears as counsel for opposing party, respondent Paul Miyamoto

The cause is not contested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Real Party In Interest Motion To Quash is DENIED. The minor discrepancy in the title of the court in the petition and summons did not render service defective. The title of the court is adequately provided per Code of Civil Procedure section 412.20(a)(1). (See Sakaguchi v. Sakaguchi (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 852 [misspelling of defendant's name in summons did not render it defective].) As petitioner notes, the real parties had no problem filing their motion to quash with the correct court. "The provisions of this chapter should be liberally construed to effectuate service and uphold the jurisdiction of the court if actual notice has been received by the defendant, and in the last analysis the question of service should be resolved by considering each situation from a practical standpoint." (Hammer Collections Co. v. Ironsides Computer Corp. (1985) 172 Cal.App.3d 899, 902.

The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com.

Petitioner Andina Property Llc Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Writ Is Transferred To Department 302 To Be Heard On October 22, 2020 At 9:30 A.M. Per The Order Of The Presiding Judge. Motion To Quash Shall Be Heard At The Same Time In Department 302.
Off Calendar Oct-29-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Daniel Bornstein of the law offices of Bornstein Law (daniel@bornstein.law) appears as counsel for moving party, petitioner Andina Property LLC

Attorney, Paul J. Katz of the law offices of KATZ APPELLATE LAW (paul@katzappellatelaw.com) appears as CO-counsel for moving party, petitioner Andina Property LLC

Attorney, Wayne K. Snodgrass, Deputy City Attorney of the San Francisco City Attorney's Office (wayne.snodgrass@sfgov.org) appears as counsel for opposing party, respondent Paul Miyamoto

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides on record as reflected in the notes of the Certified Shorthand Reporter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Petitioner Andina Property LLc's Motion For Judgment On The Writ is DENIED. "A writ of mandate may be issued against a public body or public officer 'to compel the performance of an act which the law specially enjoins, as a duty resulting from an office, trust, or station' in cases 'where there is not a plain, speedy, and adequate remedy, in the ordinary course of law.'" (Flores v. Dept. of Corrections and Rehabilitation (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 199, 205; Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1086.) The general rule is that a writ will not issue if such a remedy exists. (Id.) In CUD-19-664902, petitioner applied for the exact same relief that he seeks in this case. On July 8, 2020, Judge Haines denied petitioner's motion for writ of mandate and ruled that there is a "plain, speedy, and adequate remedy in the ordinary course of law available to Plaintiff/Petitioner." For this Court to now grant petitioner relief, it would have to implicitly overrule Judge Haines' prior ruling. Except in limited circumstances, however, one superior court judge may not overrule another superior court judge. (See In re Marriage of Oliverez (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 1242, 1247 ["Generally, one trial court judge may not reconsider and overrule an interim ruling of another trial judge."]; People v. Goodwillie (2007) 147 Cal.App.4th 695, 713 ["The rule that one superior court judge may not reconsider the previous ruling of another superior court judge applies in a variety of settings, in both criminal and civil cases."]; In re Alberto (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 421, 427 ["For one superior court judge, no matter how well intended, even if correct as a matter of law, to nullify a duly made, erroneous ruling of another superior court judge places the second judge in the role of a one-judge appellate court."] A second judge may revisit a prior judge's ruling only where the judgment is reversed and remanded for a new trial, the first judge is unavailable, or where the order was the result of inadvertence, mistake, or fraud. (Goodwillie, 147 Cal.App.4th at 713-714.) Here, none of those circumstances is present. This court cannot review Judge Haines's determination even if it believes, as a matter of law, that a separate action was necessary to compel the Sheriff to act. "[B]ecause a superior court is but one tribunal, an order 'made in one department during the progress of a cause can neither be ignored nor overlooked in another department ....'" (In re Alberto, 102 Cal.App.4th at 428.) Written order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
